title: Limits of Static Embeddings
concept: Static embeddings assign ONE fixed vector per word, failing to handle homonyms (e.g., 'bank' as a river vs a financial institution). Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Subword Tokenization.'

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of static word embeddings?

options.text: They assign one fixed vector to each word, failing to handle multiple meanings.
They only work on numbers and cannot process text.
They constantly change their vocabulary every time you run them.

exercises.explain: Static embeddings assign a single, permanent vector to each word, so they cannot handle words with multiple meanings (homonyms) very well.

cards.title: One Word, Many Faces
cards.body: If you hear the word 'apple', do you think of a crunchy red fruit or a shiny new smartphone? Humans can instantly tell the difference based on the sentence, but older AI models get completely tripped up! Let's find out why.

cards.title: What are Static Embeddings?
cards.body: Static embeddings assign exactly ONE fixed vector (a list of numbers) to each word. Because this vector never changes, the model cannot tell the difference when a word has multiple meanings depending on the context.

cards.title: The Name Tag Mix-up
cards.body: Imagine giving every person in town a name tag that says 'Chris'. If someone shouts 'Hey Chris!', you won't know if they want Chris the baker, Chris the doctor, or Chris the builder. Static embeddings do the exact same thing by giving 'bank' a single vector tag, ignoring if it's the riverbank or the money bank!

cards.title: The 'Bank' Dilemma
cards.body:
Consider these two sentences:
1. 'I deposited money in the bank.'
2. 'We sat on the grassy river bank.'

A static embedding model uses the exact same mathematical vector for 'bank' in both sentences, completely missing the change in meaning!

cards.title: Myth: AI Always Understands Context
cards.body: It's easy to think that because AI models are complex, they can figure out context automatically. But static embeddings are strictly look-up tables. If a word has one vector in the table, it stays that way forever, no matter what other words are next to it!

cards.title: Static vs. Contextual
cards.body: See how a static system cannot adapt, while a human (or dynamic system) splits the meanings based on the situation.

left.text: 'bank' = Vector [0.4, -0.1, 0.9] (Used for both money and rivers)

exercises.prompt: Why do static embeddings struggle with words like 'bank' or 'crane'?

options.text: They are too slow to calculate in real time.
They only work for short English words.
They give homonyms (like 'bark') the exact same vector.
They refuse to store words that have more than five letters.

exercises.explain: Homonyms are words that are spelled the same but have different meanings. Static embeddings fail here because they cannot change the vector based on context.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Static embeddings change their representation of a word depending on the words around it.
exercises.explain: Because static embeddings assign one vector per word, they cannot adjust the meaning based on the surrounding sentence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about static embeddings.
exercises.sentence: Static embeddings assign exactly one fixed _______ to each word, regardless of how it is used.
exercises.explain: Static embeddings only look at the word itself to find its vector, completely ignoring the surrounding context words.

exercises.prompt: Which of these sounds like the work of a static embedding model?

options.text: An AI model that representing 'light' differently in 'light bulb' and 'light box'.
An AI model that represents 'light weight' and 'turn on the light' with the exact same vector.
An AI model that flags 'light' as a spelling error.

exercises.explain: The first option shows a dynamic, context-aware understanding. The second option shows a static model giving the exact same representation to two different meanings.

exercises.prompt: How does a static embedding model handle a word with two totally different meanings, like 'bat' (the animal vs. the baseball tool)?

options.text: By assigning multiple different vectors to each word based on its letters
By mixing both meanings together into a single, confusing average vector
By ignoring the word entirely and skipping it during training
By deleting one of the meanings from the vocabulary dictionary

exercises.explain: Awesome work! You understand that static embeddings give one fixed vector per word, meaning they fail to adapt to the surrounding context. Next up: Subword Tokenization.
